<!-- This is also a comment and generally won't be displayed. Use Ctrl+Fn+shift+PgDn (Ctrl+Shift+End) to select lines below a highlighted line. -->


# **4\. Mechanical Ground Support Equipment**

## **4.1 Scope of Project**

### 4.1.1 Problem

The development process of a CubeSat involves the following major stages:

* Assembly and Integration;  
* Qualification and Testing (TVAC and Vibration Testing according to ECSS/Exolaunch requirements);  
* Delivery to the Launch Integrator (Exolaunch).

Specialised Mechanical Ground Support Equipment (MGSE) are required to support each process stage. For Galassia-5, MGSE must be custom-designed to support the in-house customised frame, external deployable solar panels and antennae, and accommodate testing and transportation requirements (e.g. Interfaces to testing equipment).

### 4.1.2 Objective/Design Statement

* Develop in-house, dedicated MGSE custom-designed for Galassia-5 that **fully supports all phases** of the CubeSat development process, including assembly, integration, environmental qualification, and transportation to the launch site.   
* The MGSE shall be **specifically tailored** to the custom frame, deployables, and testing/transportation requirements of G5, and **enable safe handling of G5 from cleanroom through to launch vehicle integration**.  
* Provide a blueprint for MGSE development in future NUS Galassia missions ![][image1]

*Fig. 4.1.1.  CubeSat Development Timeline with corresponding MGSE*

## **4.2 Combined Assembly and TVAC Test Jig**

### 4.2.1. Design Requirements

The Combined Assembly and TVAC Test Jig (used for the Assembly, Integration, and TVAC testing of the Galassia-5 satellite) must fulfill the following operational requirements:

* **Dimensions/Orientation:** Accommodate Galassia-5 (6U) in all 3 orientations.   
* **Load Capacity:**  Carry Galassia-5’s full weight (approximately 9kg) plus safety margin.  
*  **ESD Safe:** Parts that contact Galassia-5 must be ESD-safe to prevent buildup and sudden discharge of static electricity, which can damage sensitive onboard electronics. ESD-safe materials ensure any charge is safely dissipated..  
* **Low Outgassing:** Materials must meet ECSS low-outgassing requirements as the jig will be under vacuum in the TVAC chamber. Outgassing occurs when materials release trapped/volatile gases while in vacuum, which can condense onto critical surfaces such as optics and sensors, possibly degrading instrument performance.   
* **Portable within Cleanroom:** Jig is lightweight for portability.  
* **Clear Access to underside**: Provide underside clearance to the satellite bottom for integration and component testing.  
* **Ease of manufacture:** Simple to manufacture, assemble, and cost-effective compared to market options.

### 4**.**2.2. Detailed Design of Combined Assembly and TVAC Test Jig

Throughout the prototyping iterations, we continuously updated and improved our overall design. We arrived at our final design after completing three main iterations. More information on the Concept Generation/Material Selection process is available in Appendix \_\_ (copy from interim).

![][image2]

The final base plate is constructed from Al6061 tooling plate with dimensions of 420 mm x 270 mm x 12.7 mm. MIC-6 was specifically chosen because it is cast aluminum with pre-processed flat surface, eliminating the need for any additional post-processing or precision milling to achieve flatness.

This size allowance provides enough space for the following modular stands to be securely mounted, ensuring that all three primary orientations (X, Y, and Z axes) of the Galassia-5 satellite can be adequately supported.

![][image3]![][image4]

* **Corner Towers (Vertical Orientation):** Vertical ESD-ABS posts that interfaces with CubeSat rails at all 4 corners of the CubeSat, holding Galassia-5 in vertical orientation.   
* **Rail Blocks (Horizontal Orientation):** These consist of low-profile ESD-safe ABS blocks that interface with the CubeSat's side rails. The rail blocks are spaced sufficiently far apart so that the rails at either edge of the CubeSat can fit snugly into the resulting gap, thereby holding Galassia-5 securely in a horizontal orientation.


The stands are raised to allow a 50 mm clearance beneath the CubeSat for unobstructed access to bottom components. The stands are screwed in from above to allow easy removal and installation.

To **facilitate precise alignment during assembly**, engraving for positions of stands done on the base plate. This ensured that the screw holes would align accurately when the stands were placed into position.

To **prevent the CubeSat from toppling/sliding** off the rail blocks during transport in horizontal orientation, the rail holders on the left side feature integrated stoppers, and endblocks were designed to be installed onto the ends of the right-side rail blocks using M3 screws.

Ergonomic handles are also installed on the jig for easy transport within the cleanroom. Since permanent handles may obstruct access to Galassia-5 from certain angles, the handles can be easily removed/installed using easily accessible M5 screws.

![][image5]

### 4**.**2.3. Simulation and Testing

To ensure that holes cut into the material (for weight reduction) would not negatively affect the structural integrity of the base plate when subjected to static loads from Galassia-5, a Finite Element Analysis (FEA) was conducted. During this simulation, a total distributed load of 240N (twice the maximum possible load) was applied with the handles fixed, and a maximum stress of 5.27 \*10^6 N/m^2 was recorded. This is far below yield stress of Al6061 (2.76\*10^8 N/m^2) or ESD-ABS (3.2x10^7 N/m^2), thereby validating the safety of the design.

Following the finalization of the design, the jig was manufactured, and a fit test conducted using a structural model of Galassia-5. The satellite successfully fit onto the jig as intended.

### 4.2.4. Evaluation

Ultimately, the jig design demonstrates several unique features:

* Its modular design **supports all 3 orientations** of Galassia-5, providing flexibility during integration and testing.   
* The jig is also **Lightweight** compared to other similar Jigs on the market, weighing only 2.6 kg compared to Endurosat’s 3.5 kg (EnduroSat, n.d.).  
* The design supports **testing of deployable components** by providing sufficient spatial clearance around critical areas.   
* The Jig utilises materials with low outgassing properties, making it **suitable for use in TVAC environments**, eliminating the need for another Jig solely designed for TVAC use.   
* The endblocks allow G5 to be securely fastened during transport in cleanroom, **preventing toppling or sliding.**

## **4.3 Vibration Jig**

During launch, CubeSats are subjected to severe dynamic loads, necessitating rigorous vibration testing. For the G5 mission, qualification of the Flight Model (FM) is performed using the Exolaunch Nova Testpod. However, the Nova Testpod is highly expensive and typically only available for rent for short durations during the final qualification phase.

This presents a critical problem for the preliminary testing of the G5 Engineering Model (EM). Without a dedicated test fixture, the team cannot perform iterative design validation or identify structural flaws early in the development cycle. Secondary market research reveals that commercial test pods can cost tens of thousands of dollars to procure. Hence, the in-house design and fabrication of a custom vibration testing jig is needed.

### 4.3.1. Design Specifications

The vibration jig (for preliminary testing of G5 EM) must fulfil the following requirements:

* **Dimensions:** Accommodate the Galassia-5 EM, providing adequate clearance for X-face antennae/deployables, and Y-face radiation shielding (4–5 mm) and solar panels (3 mm).  
* **Modal Frequency:** The first fundamental resonance frequency must be above 1000 Hz. This ensures rigid-body behavior, preventing low-frequency resonance from amplifying destructive energy into the CubeSat.  
* **Transfer Function:** Require rigid clamping to achieve a transfer function near 1.0, maximizing energy transfer from the shaker and preventing micro-slip or chattering.  
* **Mass Optimization:** Minimize dead mass to reduce the total mass that must be accelerated by the shaker table and facilitate transport.  
* **Interface Compatibility:** Base plate must physically match the bolt patterns of shaker tables at shortlisted test facilities (currently TUV SUD and ST Satsys).  
* **Test Standard Compliance:** Withstand 3-axis sine sweeps and random vibrations, meeting SpaceX rideshare PSD requirements (5.57 GRMS loading).

### 4.3.2. Concept Generation and Prototyping 

Two primary concepts were generated through a literature review and subsequently CAD model **prototypes** were made and evaluated.

![][image6]

\<photo of sim 20mm\>

**Concept A: Enclosed Testpod Design:** Modelled after Exolaunch Nova and ESA PhiSat deployers, this uses a fully enclosed aluminum box, usually welded Al plates or milled from a large block of stock material. 

A CAD model prototype of Concept A was produced and it was noted that 20mm was the minimum wall thickness required to achieve 1st modal frequency above 1000Hz. Based on Solidworks Mass Properties, the empty jig weighs over 40kg. Substantial raw material is required for manufacturing, the heavy structure reduces portability, requires welding capabilities that NUS does not possess, and it is difficult to design a clamping mechanism within a closed assembly to internally clamp the rails of the CubeSat.

\<picture of 2 bolts assy w/ simulation\>

\<3bolt assy w/ sim\>

**Concept B: Open-Frame Clamp Assembly:** Inspired by the OreSat jig, this design clamps the CubeSat to the shaker table between two plates using bolts. It requires significantly less material, eliminates welding through a bolted design, and offers modularity for easy transport.

A CAD model prototype of Concept B was produced, initially using 2 bolts. The lowest modal frequency was \_\_\_\_. Simulation showed that the first modal frequency crossed 1000Hz when three bolts on each side were used (total 6 bolts). To incorporate a safety factor and also allow for more even clamping of the CubeSat rail, the final design used ten bolts.

After evaluating both prototypes/concepts, and after consultation with the Bus Contractor (NuSpace), Concept B was selected for its manufacturability, lower mass, and ease of clamping.

The choice of material was mainly influenced by elastic modulus and density:

* Plastics: Rejected; their elastic modulus is too low to survive the high vibration loads without deforming.  
* Steel provides high stiffness, but its high density (7850 kg/m³) would make the assembly very heavy.  
* Aluminum 6061 (Al6061) was chosen. It delivers an ideal strength-to-weight ratio (density 2700 kg/m³), excellent machinability, and a sufficient elastic modulus (\~69 GPa) to push the fundamental resonance frequency above the 1000 Hz requirement.

### 4.3.3 Detailed Design

\<CAD Assy\>

In the final detailed design, the G5 EM is clamped between a top plate and a base plate using ten M6 bolts.

* The **top plate** features cutouts to accommodate the rails as well as the externally mounted solar panels on the Y face.  
* The **round base plate**, measuring 550 mm in diameter, features M10 counterbore holes designed to interface with both the TUV SUD and ST Satsys shaker tables. Mechanical drawings of the shaker tables were obtained to ensure correct hole placements.  
* A minimum material thickness of 20 mm is maintained throughout the plates to ensure **structural rigidity** and to resist any bending moments induced by the shaker armature.  
* **Design for Manufacturability (DFM):** Instead of machining the rail holders directly out of the base plate, which would require a long machining time and generate excessive material waste, the rail holders were designed as separate modules that bolt directly onto the base plate.  
* Sufficient **clearance** is provided along both the X and Y axes to safely house all externally mounted deployables/solar panels.

### 4.3.4. Simulation and Testing Scope

Prior to physical prototyping, Finite Element Analysis (FEA) was conducted on the design.

\<sim photo\>

Using Solidworks Frequency Simulation, Simulation results confirmed that the first fundamental resonance frequency remained well above the 1000 Hz threshold, thereby fulfilling the resonance frequency requirement. 

**Based on Solidworks Mass Properties,** the empty jig mass is approximately 23 kg. When combined with the estimated 8.7 kg mass of the G5 EM, the total dynamic mass mounted to the shaker table is roughly 32 kg. This total falls safely within the operational limits of the intended shaker tables. When split into individual parts, each part of the jig does not exceed 15kg, ensuring user-centricness as the parts are human-portable and easy to install.

Prove that simulation accurate?

Any other simulation to be done?

At the time of writing, the physical jig is under production and will arrive in Week 12\. Thereafter, a fit test will be conducted with the G5 structural model. This website will be updated after the fit test is conducted.

### 4.3.5. Evaluation

* The modular design of the jig allows easy installation/takedown, and the parts are sufficiently lightweight for user transport.  
* Less material used due to the jig’s open-frame design  
* The design allows easy clamping of CubeSat rails, a specific force can be applied by adjusting the bolt torque.  
* The vibration jig provides a relatively low-cost solution, bypassing the logistical and financial constraints of renting the Nova Testpod for EM testing. This solution is easily repeatable for vibration testing of future CubeSats developed by NUS.

### 4.3.6. Shortcomings and Future Work

**Bolt Relaxation:** The jig relies entirely on M6 bolted joints. Under high vibration, localized plastic deformation beneath the bolt heads or loosening of threads can reduce pre-load, causing destructive chatter (impacting the test results).

* *Future Work:* Use mechanical retention like Belleville washers, lockwire, or stainless-steel inserts to maintain bolt torque. Chemical thread lockers (e.g. Loctite) may complicate jig removal/installation.

**Simulations Limitations:** Although FEA predicts resonance above 1000 Hz, but simulations may not capture real-world dynamics perfectly.

* *Future Work:* Perform a low-level continuous sine sweep on the empty jig to physically validate FEA results and rule out sub-1000Hz resonances before loading the G5 EM.

## 

## **4.4 CubeSat Transport Case**

Transporting Galassia-5 to the launch integrator (Exolaunch) requires safe (cushioned from shock/impact) and dust-free transport conditions.

Based on past Galassia missions, the CubeSat is placed in a transport case, which is then placed in a cushioned COTS Pelican Case to protect against shock loads during shipping. Hence, a customised transport case needs to be designed and fabricated for G5, which will then be placed in a suitable Pelican Case for shipping. 

### 4.4.1. Design Specifications

The CubeSat transport case must fulfill the following requirements:

* **Capacity & Dimensions:** Fully accommodate Galassia-5, including all externally mounted antennae, solar panels and deployables.  
* **Environmental Control:** Even though Pelican Cases are dust proof, the transport case should be dust-resistant to prevent particulate contamination when the Pelican Case is opened, which can degrade sensitive optics and electronics  
* **ESD-Safe:** Must be ESD-safe to allow engineers to safely power on the satellite while it remains secured inside the case.  
* **Portability (Handling):** Must be easily portable and feature integrated carry handles. The handles should be easily removable/installable.  
* **Transparent:** Transparent case allows visual inspection of the CubeSat without needing to remove the satellite from the case.  
* **Accessibility:** Must feature a dedicated slot to access the Flight Preparation Panel (FPP) without needing to remove the entire satellite from the case, to facilitate power-on tests and battery charging.

### 4.4.2. Concept Generation and Prototyping

Aluminum, standard polycarbonate, and AC-300 ESD-safe acrylic were evaluated for the exterior panels. 

| Evaluation Criteria | Aluminum Panels | Standard Polycarbonate | AC-300 ESD-Safe Acrylic |
| :---- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **ESD-Safe (for power on of CubeSat) (**(10⁶ to 10⁸ Ω/sq)) | ✔ |  | ✔ |
| **Transparency** (Visual inspection capability) |  | ✔ | ✔ |
| **Weight** (Lighter is better) |  | ✔ | ✔ |
| **Strength** | ✔ |  |  |
| Decision |  |  | ✔ |

For the internal guide rails, metal rods were initially considered but ultimately rejected due to the risk of metal-on-metal particulate generation when sliding against the aluminum CubeSat rails. Instead, ESD-safe polymers (PEEK/ABS/POM) were evaluated. ESD-ABS was selected for its ease of manufacturing via 3D printing. This material also provides an ideal surface resistivity of 10⁶ to 10⁸ Ω/sq.

\<10mm acrylic assy photo\>

\<sim of diff thickness\>

A CAD prototype consisting of 10mm AC-300 acrylic was produced. It was found to be too heavy, with the empty case already weighing 7 kg. After static load simulations of different acrylic thickness, 5mm thickness was found to be sufficiently strong for the use case. This reduces the empty case mass to 4.5 kg. Together with G5 mass of 8.7kg, the overall mass is 14.2 kg, which is sufficiently lightweight to be carried by 1-2 personnel.

The hole positions on the 6U face (top face) of the transport case were also altered to allow the handle to be installed directly on the same holes fixing the acrylic panels to the guide rails. One only needs to re-install the screws after installing (or removing) the handles.

### 4.4.3. Detailed Design

The 324 x 365 x 170 mm enclosure is constructed from 5mm thick laser-cut AC-300 acrylic plates, which are fastened together using M5 screws. To ensure dust resistance, the acrylic panels are designed to overlap.

![][image7]

Inside the enclosure, four ESD-ABS rods are mounted to act as the primary guide rails. These guide rails are positioned according to the exact distances required between the rails, ensuring that the CubeSat fits snugly and does not shake around within the box. 

Additionally, individual panels on the X face of the case can be removed to access the FPP. This allows for umbilical connections for power-on testing/battery charging while maintaining the integrity of the enclosure. 

![][image8]

To ensure impact resistance and protect against shock during transport, the entire CubeSat transport case assembly will be placed inside a Pelican 1650 Protector Case equipped with shock-absorbing foam customized to the dimensions of the acrylic case. Shock sensors will be placed within the Pelican Case to record the amount of shock loads experienced by G5 during transport.

![][image9]

### 4.4.4. Simulation and Testing

To validate the design, comprehensive simulation and physical testing was conducted.

1. **Finite Element Analysis (FEA):** A SolidWorks simulation was performed by applying a 24kg static load (twice the maximum mass of a 6U CubeSat) to the rails of the assembly. The results show that the CubeSat case can be safely carried by the handles and is capable of sustaining the load of the CubeSat inside.  
2. **Physical Fit and Function Test:** A prototype was fabricated. The G5 Structural Model  was used for test-fitting, and the CubeSat was able to fit with sufficient clearance for deployables and externally mounted antennae.

### 4.4.5. Evaluation

This transport case successfully met all intended deliverables and is unique for the following reasons:

* It is custom-fit to the exact dimensions of Galassia-5.  
* The ESD-safe materials allow the satellite to be powered on while inside the case.   
* The integration of a handle allows for ease of portability.  
* A dedicated provision for the FPP allows access to the FPP for connections of umbilical connectors and power-on of the CubeSat.

### 4.4.5. Recommendations and Future Work

While the overlapping joints can limit particulate ingress, the transport case is not completely dust-proof. Future iterations could incorporate O-rings or rubber gaskets to ensure sealing.

Aft blocker on rail may activate kill switch.

## **4.5. Conclusion**

The 3 custom-designed MGSE effectively address each critical stage of the Galassia-5 CubeSat development journey. 

* The Combined Assembly and TVAC Test Jig supports both integration (in all orientations) and TVAC testing without needing multiple jigs.   
* The Vibration Jig offers a cost-effective, high-resonance open-frame alternative for qualification of CubeSat Engineering Models.   
* Finally, the CubeSat Transport Case ensures safe transport while allowing access to the Flight Preparation Panel and power-on within the case which is ESD-safe. 

Together, these three customised, in-house innovations not only solve immediate logistical challenges for G5, but also establish a **robust and repeatable blueprint for MGSE in future CubeSat programs at NUS.**
